summary: I was born in a Karen village called Thay Baw Boe. It’s where my early childhood began before my family moved to a refugee camp. Growing up there shaped my identity and roots before we faced the challenges of displacement.

In 1997, when I was seven years old, my family moved to the Nu Poe Refugee Camp on the border of Burma and Thailand. We were among the first Burmese refugees in Thailand at that camp. I spent 15 years there growing up and attending school, where I received my most treasured gift, a dictionary from my high school teacher.

Burma is the country my family originally came from before we fled due to conflict. Living near the border of Burma and Thailand, we sought refuge in Thailand to escape the difficulties there. My identity as a Burmese refugee has influenced my journey and the challenges I faced growing up.

Thailand became my home when my family and I moved to the Nu Poe Refugee Camp in 1997. We were the first Burmese refugees recognized there, and I spent 15 years growing up in that camp within Thailand. It was a place of hardship but also education and hope before we eventually emigrated.

My family and I emigrated to the United States on January 28, 2012, which was a day I will never forget. Moving here represented a huge opportunity and the fulfillment of a dream for us as refugees to rebuild our lives. Since arriving, I have focused on education and personal growth, carrying with me a dictionary that symbolizes my hard work and hope.

I have been living in Minnesota for three years since arriving in the United States. This state has become my new home where I continue to pursue my education and future goals. I brought with me a dictionary from my refugee camp school, which remains a valuable reminder of my journey and the importance of hard work.
